name: Richard Rodgers
description: American composer of songs and Broadway musicals (1902–1979)
abstract: Richard Charles Rodgers (June 28, 1902 – December 30, 1979) was an American composer who worked primarily in musical theater. With 43 Broadway musicals and over 900 songs to his credit, Rodgers was one of the best-known American composers of the 20th century, and his work significantly influenced popular music.

about_quotes.speaker: Bucky Pizzarelli
about_quotes.evidence: The repertoire is Jerome Kern, Johnny Mercer, Duke Ellington, Richard Rogers, Cole Porter... people like that... buy a book on Richard Rogers’ songs
about_quotes.doc_title: Bucky Pizzarelli interviewed by Monk Rowe, Aspen, Colorado, October 11, 1997 [interview 1 of 2]

about_quotes.speaker: Steve Allen
about_quotes.evidence: giants of Cole Porter and Irving Berlin and Richard Rodgers and all those giants. They really had the gift of melody.
about_quotes.doc_title: Steve Allen interviewed by Monk Rowe, Los Angeles, California, February 15, 1999

about_quotes.speaker: Ruth & Bucky Pizzarelli
about_quotes.evidence: You get all the Richard Rodgers songs and you see hey he wrote this, he wrote that, then you build a repertoire.
about_quotes.doc_title: Ruth & Bucky Pizzarelli interviewed by Monk Rowe, Saddle River, New Jersey, January 19, 2015

about_quotes.speaker: Dave Frishberg
about_quotes.evidence: Richard Rodgers, George Gershwin, that’s what changed jazz. That’s what opened the jazz musicians’ ears.
about_quotes.doc_title: Dave Frishberg interviewed by Monk Rowe, Clearwater Beach, Florida, March 16, 2001

about_quotes.speaker: Mandel Johnny
about_quotes.evidence: Richard Rodgers and Lorenz Hart, I preferred the early Rodgers by far to the later Rodgers.
about_quotes.doc_title: Mandel Johnny — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Keith Ingham
about_quotes.evidence: And I knew these songs, and they were Richard Rogers’ tunes. The guy that sent the note up was Richard Rogers... And on the way out he wrote a note saying thanks so much for playing my tunes.
about_quotes.doc_title: Keith Ingham interviewed by Monk Rowe, Chautauqua, New York, September 12, 1997
